clean_story:
Silk architects. Liquid-to-solid engineers.

The word spider comes from Old English spīthra, meaning "one who spins"—a nod to their defining skill: turning liquid protein into threads stronger than steel of the same thickness.

A spider’s web is not just a trap; it’s a vibration map. Each strand hums with frequencies—prey struggling at 100–1,000 hertz, wind at 1–10 hertz—let the spider "hear" through its legs. The golden silk orb-weaver, Nephila clavipes, spins draglines so tough NASA studied them for space tether designs. Yet their secret isn’t strength alone: silk stretches 40% before snapping, like a bungee cord catching a falling leaf.

Hunting spiders, like the net-casting Deinopidae, don’t wait. They weave tiny nets between their legs, then strike—expanding the net 5x in 100 milliseconds to ensnare crickets mid-leap. No other predator builds its weapon during the chase.

Three related subjects are venom as liquid tools, eight-legged problem solving, and how materials shape survival.
